Stamped in purple ink text from back of photograph: Credit Will Be AppreciatedParamount Photo ByC. Kenneth Lobben

Pasted-on paper with typed text attached to back of photograph: Revised Hornpipe --Dorothy Lamour is making a picture for Paramount called "The Fleet's In," so it is only natural that a quartet of sailors should help her to knock out a fast hornpipe. It's stepped up to Jimmy Dorsey's music with the help of (left to right) Gil Lamb, Jack Chapin, Sam Bagley and Rod Cameron.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: Director/composer Victor Schertzinger died on October 26, 1941, three days before the end of the film's production. According to Hollywood Reporter news items, Ralph Murphy finished directing the film but declined screen credit "so that the film could stand as a monument to a fine musical talent." Actress Dorothy Lamour stated in her autobiography that assistant director Hal Walker finished directing the film, but no contemporary information has been found to confirm this.

title_tesim: Luis Van Rooten in Champion
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: What'll You Have? -- No matter what kind of an acting role a producer may have in mind, Luis Van Rooten, Hollywood's all-purpose actor, is the man for the job. title_tesim: Marianna and Helmut Dantine share final moments in Guerrilla Girl
caption_tesim: Helmut Dantine in "Guerrilla Girl" introducing MariannaProduced and directed by John ChristianReleased thru United Artists
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: Excerpted from the AFI listing:"Guerrilla Girl" marked Greek producer-director John Christian's first American feature release. According to a Los Angeles Times news item, the actress billed as Marianna was working as Christian's production secretary before being cast in the film. A modern source lists the working title as "Dawn Comes Late."

Greek film producer/director, Giannis Hristodoulou, was credited in the film, "Guerrilla Girl" as, John Christian. He has also been credited in other films as, Jan Christian and Ioannis Hristodoulou, as well as by his birth name, Giannis Hristodoulou.
AFI lists a release date and copyright date for the film, "Guerrilla Girl,' as January 23, 1953.

description_tesim: Director/composer Victor Schertzinger died on October 26, 1941, three days before the end of the film's production. According to Hollywood Reporter news items, Ralph Murphy finished directing the film but declined screen credit "so that the film could stand as a monument to a fine musical talent." Actress Dorothy Lamour stated in her autobiography that assistant director Hal Walker finished directing the film, but no contemporary information has been found to confirm this.

A majority of the cast appear in this publicity photograph from the film, "The Fleet's In": (l to r) Jimmie Dundee, Harry Barris, Boyd 'Red' Morgan, Rod Cameron, Dave Willock, Eddie Bracken, Jack Chapin, William Holden and Dorothy Lamour.

title_tesim: Betty Field with makeup artist Jack Pierce on the set of Flesh and Fantasy.
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: From: Universal Studios - Universal City, Calif. Returning to the screen after a long absence (during which she became a mother) Betty Field will be seen in the Charles Boyer-Julien Duvivier episodic film, "For All We Know," wearing a mask which is the counterpart of her real face. Universal makeup chief, Jack Pierce, is shown studying her facial contours in preparatory to making the mask, which is essential to the story telling of an ugly-duckling who, Cinderella-like, is transforming by her love for a man into a beautiful girl. "For All We Know" is an all-star production in which Edw. G. Robinson, Tom Mitchell, Barbara Stanwyck, Dame May Whitty, Gloria Jean, Alan Curtis, Boyer himself, Grace McDonald and others appear.

Pasted-on paper with typed text from back of photograph: From: U-I, Universal City, Calif."Francis Goes To West Point" - - A Pictorial SynopsisStarring Donald O'Connor and his famous talking mule pal, this is the third of the comedy series which has proved a box-office bonanza. Emphasizing youth in its cast and telling a story placing the mule in a likely and natural setting, it is the best of the series. Featured roles go to young U-I discoveries, Lori Nelson, Alice Kelley, Palmer Lee, Williams [sic] Reynolds. Screenplay is by Oscar Brodney, direction by Arthur Lubin, production by Leonard Goldstein for Universal-International. The story in pictures:6. But Van Allen is in love with Barbara Atwood (Lori Nelson), which Cynthia Daniels has sensed with feminine intuition. title_tesim: Robert Duvall in The Outfit
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph:MGM EDITOR'S PORTFOLIO...."THE OUTFIT" presented by MGM stars Robert Duvall, Karen Black, Joe Don Baker and Robert Ryan in a hard-hitting story of a man just out of prison, intent on avenging his brother's murder by the syndicate. Filmed on location in and around Los Angeles, "THE OUTFIT" was produced by Carter De Haven and directed by John Flynn who wrote the screenplay based on Richard Stark's novel. Jerry Fielding composed and conducted the musical score.CAPTIONS:1920-1Robert Duvall stars in MGM's "THE OUTFIT" as a man who dares take on an entire crime syndicate in seeking to avenge his brother's death.1920-2Joanna Cassidy makes her screen debut in MGM's "THE OUTFIT" in the role of a beautiful woman married to a crime boss played by Robert Ryan.1920-3Joe Don Baker stars with Robert Duvall in MGM's "THE OUTFIT", the story of a two-man war against the crime syndicate.1920-4In MGM's "THE OUTFIT", Karen Black stars as the girl friend of a man who dares take on a crime syndicate to avenge his brother's murder.1920-5Robert Duvall and Karen Black star as two people in love, bucking a crime syndicate in MGM's "THE OUTFIT".1920-6Robert Duvall (left) and Joe Don Baker (right) shoot it out with professional killers in MGM's "THE OUTFIT".1920-7Richard Jaeckel and Sheree North co-star as pawns of a crime syndicate in MGM's hard-hitting film "THE OUTFIT".1920-8Robert Ryan portrays the head of a crime syndicate and Joanna Cassidy his wife in MGM's "THE OUTFIT".1920-9Robert Duvall battles-to-the-death with a mob leader (Timothy Carey) in MGM's "THE OUTFIT".
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

description_tesim: Actress, Dorothy Malone, began her career at RKO under her real name, Dorothy Maloney. After her two-year contract was up, Warner Bros offered her a contract, where she was billed as, Dorothy Malone. She would be credited as Dorothy Malone for the rest of her long career in film and television.

A photograph of a scene from the film, "Higher and Higher," featuring a wedding ceremony containing much of the cast (identified cast members, l to r): Elaine Riley (bridesmaid, extreme left), Daun Kennedy (bridesmaid, behind and to the left of bride), Jack Chefe (in tuxedo, directly behind bride), Michèle Morgan (bride), Leon Errol (arm in arm with bride), Dorothy Malone (bridesmaid behind Errol), Shirley O'Hara (bridesmaid to right of Errol) and Tanis Chandler (bridesmaid in right foreground).

Other cast members credited as 'wedding guests', but not identified in this photograph, may include the following actors: Paul Bradley, Jay Eaton, Jack Gargan, Dick Gordon, Perc Launders, Wilbur Mack, Bert Moorhouse, Edmund Mortimer, Fred Rapport and Larry Steers.

Actress, Tanis Chandler, began her career at RKO under her real name, Anne Goldthwaite, but only in uncredited roles. Full birth name: Tanis Anne Goldthwaite, daughter of composer/soloist Chandler Goldthwaite.

title_tesim: Albert Schweitzer in "Albert Schweitzer"
caption_tesim: Jerome Hill and Erica Anderson present "Aklbert Schweitzer" in Eastmancolor. Narrated by Fredric March and Burgess Meredith.

Text from back of photograph: "Alibert Schweitzer" - Dr. Albert Schweitzer staking out new buildings for his leper colony in French Equatorial Africa. A scene from "Albert Schweitzer", the definitive film biography of the Nobel Prize winner who has achieved world-renown as a philosopher, theologian, missionary, physician, organist and, above all, as a man who has devoted his life to serving his fellow men. The Eastmancolor picture, produced and directed by Jerome Hill and photographed by Erica Anderson, took five years to make. It was filmed in Dr. Schweitzer's childhood village, Gunsbach, France and in the hospital-village in Lambarene, French Equatorial Africa. Narration by Fredric March and Burgress Meredith. Original score by Alec Wilder.

title_tesim: Martha Raye receiving a throat injection on the set of Tropic Holiday
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: HOLLYWOOD GOES SCIENTIFIC - Every new ailment is followed by a remedy, even in Hollywood, where time is money and ailments are costly. Newest scientific discovery in the film capitol is the lubrication of inflamed vocal cords in the throats of singers. Here, Martha Raye is shown on the set of Paramount's "Tropic Holiday," having a new oil formula injected into her vocal cords by Dr. Edward T. Fogel, throat specialist who perfected the treatment. The soothing effect of the injection lasts two hours, during which Martha is able to sing her "hottest" songs, and production is not delayed. All Hollywood is to follow suit, in cases of throat difficulties.

Starring Tony Curtis and Piper Laurie with Don DeFore and Spring Byington, it was written for the screen by Joseph Hoffman, directed by Douglas Sirk and produced by Ted Richmond for Universal-International. The story in pictures: 10. But Lee beats Alvah to his compartment, where he finds her with the champagne and saturated with heady perfume. title_tesim: May McAvoy and Harry Langdon in When Comedy Was King
caption_tesim: Attention entertainment editor--for release in Thursday am's 5/19. Suggested for use with Joe Finnigan story on the collection of silent films. 5/19/60--Hollywood. May McAvoy and Harry Langdon in this scene shot about 1914 gives you an idea of what you can expect in the 90-minute "When Comedy was King." The picture is a collection of some of history's greatest laugh getters shown on the silent screen. The 20th Century Fox release spliced the old movies showing such stars as Charlie Chaplin, Harry Langdon, Wallace Beery, Gloria Swanson, Laurel & Hardy, The Keystone Cops and many others.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
